=== Chūgoku Region === * {{ill|Jūōmen Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:十王免横穴群]] ({{ill|Matsue|en}}[[ja:松江市]], [[Shimane Prefecture]]) - 37 tombs * {{ill|Kitsunedani Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:狐谷横穴墓群]] (Matsue, Shimane) - 16 tombs * {{ill|Kamikonishi Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:上塩冶横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Izumo, Shimane|en}}[[ja:出雲市]], Shimane) * {{ill|Takatsuku Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:高津久横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Chibu, Shimane|en}}[[ja:知夫村]], [[Oki District]])
=== Kyushu Region === * {{ill|Takenami Site|en}}[[ja:竹並遺跡]] ({{ill|Yukuhashi|en}}[[ja:行橋市]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]) - Over 1,000 tombs * {{ill|Shiroyama Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:城山横穴群]] ({{ill|Fukuchi, Fukuoka|en}}[[ja:福智町]], Fukuoka) - National Historic Site, over 200 tombs * {{ill|Mizumachi Site|en}}[[ja:水町遺跡]] ({{ill|Nōgata|en}}[[ja:直方市]], Fukuoka) - Prefectural Historic Site, over 40 tombs * {{ill|Kozuki Yokoguchi|en}}[[ja:古月横穴]] ({{ill|Kurate, Fukuoka|en}}[[ja:鞍手町]], Fukuoka) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Ishinuki Nagino Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:石貫ナギノ横穴群]] ({{ill|Tamana|en}}[[ja:玉名市]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Ishinuki Anakanon Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:石貫穴観音横穴群]] (Tamana, Kumamoto) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Nabeta Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:鍋田横穴群]] ({{ill|Yamaga, Kumamoto|en}}[[ja:山鹿市]], Kumamoto) - National Historic Site, 60 tombs * {{ill|Ōmura Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:大村横穴群]] ({{ill|Hitoyoshi|en}}[[ja:人吉市]], Kumamoto) - National Historic Site, 27 tombs * {{ill|Yokkaichi Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:四日市横穴群]] ({{ill|Usa|en}}[[ja:宇佐市]], [[Ōita Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, 161 tombs * {{ill|Takio Hyakka Yokoguchi Kofun Cluster|en}}[[ja:滝尾百穴横穴古墳群]] ({{ill|Ōita, Ōita|en}}[[ja:大分市]], Ōita) - Municipal Historic Site, 75 tombs * {{ill|Hasunogaike Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:蓮ヶ池横穴群]] ({{ill|Miyazaki, Miyazaki|en}}[[ja:宮崎市]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site

revision.text:
{{distinguish|Yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu]] [[File:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群11号墓.jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Yōkokuji Valley Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群]] Tomb 11: House-shaped chamber.]] [[File:Yoshimi Yoshimi-hyakuana 2.JPG|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Yoshimi Hundred Caves|en}}[[ja:吉見百穴]] (November 2010)]] [[File:柏谷横穴群.JPG|right|250px|thumb|{{ill|Kashiya Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:柏谷横穴群]] (February 2013)]]
'''Yokohama Tombs''' (横穴墓, Yokōbo/Ouketsubo<ref name="rekihaku">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=https://www.rekihaku.city.yokohama.jp/maibun/qa/detail.php?seq=22|title=Q：横穴墓ってなんて読むのですか?|publisher=Yokohama City Foundation for History and Culture|accessdate=2018-07-28]]</ref>) are burial tombs generally carved into slopes or cliffs, typically measuring around 2 meters in height and extending several meters in depth. These tombs served as burial chambers in ancient East Asia and are most commonly associated with the [[Kofun period]] in Japan, where this burial type emerged and became widely used.
== Characteristics == [[File:市ヶ尾横穴墓群B群.JPG|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Ichigao Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:市ヶ尾横穴墓群]] B Cluster: Forecourt and entrance.]] [[File:出山横穴墓群 2009.03.21 - panoramio (5).jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Izuyama Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:出山横穴墓群]]: Interior view of the burial chamber.]] [[File:長柄横穴群11号墓棺座.JPG|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Nagara Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:長柄横穴群]] Tomb 11: Stone coffin base.]] Yokohama tombs closely resemble [[kofun]] tombs with horizontal stone chambers. They were carved into rock faces, featuring entrances leading to passageways (羨道, sendō), which open into burial chambers (玄室, genshitsu). These tombs typically lack burial mounds but include exceptions. Burial chambers often feature bases for coffins (棺座, kanza) and showcase various ceiling styles, such as house-shaped, dome-shaped, or arch-shaped. Some tombs incorporate forecourts and intermediate rooms between the passageway and chamber.<ref name="#1">{{Cite book|language=ja|author=Keiji Nagahara|editor=Eiichi Ishigami|title=Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1999|isbn=4-00-080093-0]]</ref>
In {{ill|Kamakura|en}}[[ja:鎌倉市]], Yokohama tombs feature the distinctive "Kamakura-type" (鎌倉型横穴墓), where burial shelves or smaller inner chambers were carved into the rear walls of burial chambers.<ref name="#2">{{Cite book|language=ja|author=Sakae Ward Historical Committee|title=The History of Sakae|publisher=Sakae Ward Community Promotion Division|year=2013]]</ref>
Yokohama tombs are typically found in clusters (横穴墓群, yokohama bokun). The {{ill|Yoshimi Hundred Caves|en}}[[ja:吉見百穴]] in {{ill|Yoshimi|en}}[[ja:吉見町]], {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en}}[[ja:埼玉県]], comprises over 200 tombs and is a prominent example designated as a National Historic Site.
Some tombs also feature wall carvings and paintings, often categorized as decorative kofun. Colored embellishments have been discovered in regions such as [[Kyushu]], [[Kanto]], and parts of [[Tohoku]].
== Origins and Evolution == Yokohama tombs originated in northern Kyushu around the late 5th century during the Kofun period and subsequently spread across western Japan, including the San'in, Sanyo, Kinki, and Tokai regions by the mid-6th century. By the early 7th century, they had reached the Hokuriku, Kanto, and southern Tohoku regions. The practice peaked around the time of the {{ill|Thin Burial Edict|en}}[[ja:薄葬令]] before declining in the late 8th century.<ref name="#1"/>
== Name == Yokohama tombs are sometimes called "yokoana kofun" (横穴古墳), but technically the term "kofun" refers to burial mounds, which these tombs lack. They are often grouped under broader definitions of kofun but differ significantly in their construction and purpose.<ref name="rekihaku"/>
The term "yokohama tombs" became common in the 1960s, gaining popularity among researchers in eastern Japan, whereas western Japan researchers traditionally referred to them as "yokoana." The term "yokohama" is preferred for consistency.<ref name="rekihaku"/>
== Distribution and Notable Yokoguchi Tombs == These tombs are found from Kyushu through San'in, Kinki, Hokuriku, and Tokai, with a high concentration in [[Southern Kantō]]. The northern limit is thought to be in northern [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. Approximately 3,000 examples exist in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]].

=== Kyushu Region === * {{ill|Takenami Site|en}}[[ja:竹並遺跡]] ({{ill|Yukuhashi|en}}[[ja:行橋市]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]) - Over 1,000 tombs * {{ill|Shiroyama Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:城山横穴群]] ({{ill|Fukuchi, Fukuoka|en}}[[ja:福智町]], Fukuoka) - National Historic Site, over 200 tombs * {{ill|Mizumachi Site|en}}[[ja:水町遺跡]] ({{ill|Nōgata|en}}[[ja:直方市]], Fukuoka) - Prefectural Historic Site, over 40 tombs * {{ill|Kozuki Yokoguchi|en}}[[ja:古月横穴]] ({{ill|Kurate, Fukuoka|en}}[[ja:鞍手町]], Fukuoka) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Ishinuki Nagino Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:石貫ナギノ横穴群]] ({{ill|Tamana|en}}[[ja:玉名市]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Ishinuki Anakanon Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:石貫穴観音横穴群]] (Tamana, Kumamoto) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Nabeta Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:鍋田横穴群]] ({{ill|Yamaga, Kumamoto|en}}[[ja:山鹿市]], Kumamoto) - National Historic Site, 60 tombs * {{ill|Ōmura Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:大村横穴群]] ({{ill|Hitoyoshi|en}}[[ja:人吉市]], Kumamoto) - National Historic Site, 27 tombs * {{ill|Yokkaichi Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:四日市横穴群]] ({{ill|Usa|en}}[[ja:宇佐市]], [[Ōita Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, 161 tombs * {{ill|Takio Hyakka Yokoguchi Kofun Cluster|en}}[[ja:滝尾百穴横穴古墳群]] ({{ill|Ōita, Ōita|en}}[[ja:大分市]], Ōita) - Municipal Historic Site, 75 tombs * {{ill|Hasunogaike Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:蓮ヶ池横穴群]] ({{ill|Miyazaki, Miyazaki|en}}[[ja:宮崎市]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site
== Related Articles == * [[Kofun period]] * [[Decorated kofun|Decorated Kofun]]
== References == * Nagahara, Keiji (1999). Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary. Iwanami Shoten. * Yokohama History Foundation (2013). History of Yokohama Tombs.

revision.comment: fixing links and categorization
revision.text:
{{distinguish|Yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu]] [[File:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群11号墓.jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Yōkokuji Valley Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群]] Tomb 11: House-shaped chamber.]] [[File:Yoshimi Yoshimi-hyakuana 2.JPG|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Yoshimi Hundred Caves|en}}[[ja:吉見百穴]] (November 2010)]] [[File:柏谷横穴群.JPG|right|250px|thumb|{{ill|Kashiya Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:柏谷横穴群]] (February 2013)]]
'''Yokohama Tombs''' (横穴墓, Yokōbo/Ouketsubo<ref name="rekihaku">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=https://www.rekihaku.city.yokohama.jp/maibun/qa/detail.php?seq=22|title=Q：横穴墓ってなんて読むのですか?|publisher=Yokohama City Foundation for History and Culture|accessdate=2018-07-28]]</ref>) are burial tombs generally carved into slopes or cliffs, typically measuring around 2 meters in height and extending several meters in depth. These tombs served as burial chambers in ancient East Asia and are most commonly associated with the [[Kofun period]] in Japan, where this burial type emerged and became widely used.
== Characteristics == [[File:市ヶ尾横穴墓群B群.JPG|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Ichigao Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:市ヶ尾横穴墓群]] B Cluster: Forecourt and entrance.]] [[File:出山横穴墓群 2009.03.21 - panoramio (5).jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Izuyama Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:出山横穴墓群]]: Interior view of the burial chamber.]] [[File:長柄横穴群11号墓棺座.JPG|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Nagara Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:長柄横穴群]] Tomb 11: Stone coffin base.]] Yokohama tombs closely resemble [[kofun]] tombs with horizontal stone chambers. They were carved into rock faces, featuring entrances leading to passageways (羨道, sendō), which open into burial chambers (玄室, genshitsu). These tombs typically lack burial mounds but include exceptions. Burial chambers often feature bases for coffins (棺座, kanza) and showcase various ceiling styles, such as house-shaped, dome-shaped, or arch-shaped. Some tombs incorporate forecourts and intermediate rooms between the passageway and chamber.<ref name="#1">{{Cite book|language=ja|author=Keiji Nagahara|editor=Eiichi Ishigami|title=Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1999|isbn=4-00-080093-0]]</ref>
In {{ill|Kamakura|en}}[[ja:鎌倉市]], Yokohama tombs feature the distinctive "Kamakura-type" (鎌倉型横穴墓), where burial shelves or smaller inner chambers were carved into the rear walls of burial chambers.<ref name="#2">{{Cite book|language=ja|author=Sakae Ward Historical Committee|title=The History of Sakae|publisher=Sakae Ward Community Promotion Division|year=2013]]</ref>
Yokohama tombs are typically found in clusters (横穴墓群, yokohama bokun). The {{ill|Yoshimi Hundred Caves|en}}[[ja:吉見百穴]] in {{ill|Yoshimi|en}}[[ja:吉見町]], {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en}}[[ja:埼玉県]], comprises over 200 tombs and is a prominent example designated as a National Historic Site.
Some tombs also feature wall carvings and paintings, often categorized as decorative kofun. Colored embellishments have been discovered in regions such as [[Kyushu]], [[Kanto]], and parts of [[Tohoku]].
== Origins and Evolution == Yokohama tombs originated in northern Kyushu around the late 5th century during the Kofun period and subsequently spread across western Japan, including the San'in, Sanyo, Kinki, and Tokai regions by the mid-6th century. By the early 7th century, they had reached the Hokuriku, Kanto, and southern Tohoku regions. The practice peaked around the time of the {{ill|Thin Burial Edict|en}}[[ja:薄葬令]] before declining in the late 8th century.<ref name="#1"/>
== Name == Yokohama tombs are sometimes called "yokoana kofun" (横穴古墳), but technically the term "kofun" refers to burial mounds, which these tombs lack. They are often grouped under broader definitions of kofun but differ significantly in their construction and purpose.<ref name="rekihaku"/>
The term "yokohama tombs" became common in the 1960s, gaining popularity among researchers in eastern Japan, whereas western Japan researchers traditionally referred to them as "yokoana." The term "yokohama" is preferred for consistency.<ref name="rekihaku"/>
== Distribution and Notable Yokoguchi Tombs == These tombs are found from Kyushu through San'in, Kinki, Hokuriku, and Tokai, with a high concentration in [[Southern Kantō]]. The northern limit is thought to be in northern [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. Approximately 3,000 examples exist in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]].
=== Tōhoku Region === * {{ill|Yamahata Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:山畑横穴群]] ({{ill|Ōsaki, Miyagi|en}}[[ja:大崎市]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, 26 tombs * {{ill|Atagoyama Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:愛宕山横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Taihaku-ku, Sendai|en}}[[ja:太白区]], {{ill|Sendai|en}}[[ja:仙台市]]) * {{ill|Mogasaki Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:茂ヶ崎横穴墓群]] (Taihaku-ku, Sendai) - 25 tombs, no longer extant * {{ill|Sōzenji Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:宗禅寺横穴墓群]] (Taihaku-ku, Sendai) * {{ill|Kiyotosaku Yokoguchi|en}}[[ja:清戸迫横穴墓]] ({{ill|Futaba, Fukushima|en}}[[ja:双葉町]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, over 300 tombs * {{ill|Izumizaki Yokoguchi|en}}[[ja:泉崎横穴墓]] ({{ill|Nishishirakawa District|en}}[[ja:西白河郡]], {{ill|Izumizaki, Fukushima|en}}[[ja:泉崎村]]) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Nakata Cave Tombs|en}}[[ja:中田横穴]] ({{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|en}}[[ja:いわき市]]) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Hayama Yokoguchi|en}}[[ja:羽山横穴]] ({{ill|Minamisōma|en}}[[ja:南相馬市]], Fukushima Prefecture) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Wada Daibutsu and Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:和田大仏及び横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Sukagawa|en}}[[ja:須賀川市]], Fukushima Prefecture) - Municipal Historic Site
=== Kantō Region === * {{ill|Jūgorō-ana Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:十五郎穴横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Hitachinaka, Ibaraki|en}}[[ja:ひたちなか市]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]]) - Over 300 tombs * {{ill|Karagoshō Yokoguchi|en}}[[ja:唐御所横穴]] ({{ill|Nasukaragawa, Tochigi|en}}[[ja:那珂川町]], [[Tochigi Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, part of the Hokōda and Wami Yokoguchi Clusters * {{ill|Nagaoka Hyakka|en}}[[ja:長岡百穴古墳]] ({{ill|Utsunomiya|en}}[[ja:宇都宮市]], Tochigi Prefecture) - 52 tombs * {{ill|Yoshimi Hyakka|en}}[[ja:吉見百穴]] ({{ill|Yoshimi, Saitama|en}}[[ja:吉見町]], [[Saitama Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, over 200 tombs * {{ill|Kuroiwa Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:黒岩横穴墓群]] (Yoshimi, Saitama) - Over 30 tombs * {{ill|Jūrō Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:十郎横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Hatoyama, Saitama|en}}[[ja:鳩山町]], Saitama) - 3 tombs * {{ill|Takinojō Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:滝之城横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Tokorozawa|en}}[[ja:所沢市]], Saitama) - 9 tombs * {{ill|Kitaakitsu Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:北秋津横穴墓群]] (Tokorozawa, Saitama) - 8 tombs * {{ill|Bikuniyama Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:比丘尼山横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Higashimatsuyama|en}}[[ja:東松山市]], Saitama) - Over 50 tombs * {{ill|Takane Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:高根横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Kumagaya|en}}[[ja:熊谷市]], Saitama) - Municipal Historic Site * {{ill|Nagara Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:長柄横穴群]] ({{ill|Nagara, Chiba|en}}[[ja:長柄町]], [[Chiba Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, over 300 tombs * {{ill|Deyama Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:出山横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Mitaka, Tokyo|en}}[[ja:三鷹市]], [[Tokyo]]) - Metropolitan Historic Site, 10 tombs * {{ill|Nakawada Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:中和田横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Tama, Tokyo|en}}[[ja:多摩市]], Tokyo) - 14 tombs * {{ill|Todoroki Ravine Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:谷沢川横穴群]] ({{ill|Setagaya|en}}[[ja:世田谷区]], Tokyo) * {{ill|Ekoda Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:荏子田横穴]] ({{ill|Aoba-ku, Yokohama|en}}[[ja:青葉区]], {{ill|Yokohama|en}}[[ja:横浜市]], [[Kanagawa Prefecture]]) - Municipal Historic Site, 2 tombs * {{ill|Ichigao Yokoguchi Kofun Cluster|en}}[[ja:市ヶ尾横穴古墳群]] (Aoba-ku, Yokohama) - Prefectural Historic Site, 19 tombs * {{ill|Kanazawa River Basin Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:㹨川流域横穴墓群]] (Kamakura-style tomb concentration) ** {{ill|Matsugasaki Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:松ヶ崎横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Kōnan-ku, Yokohama|en}}[[ja:港南区]], Yokohama) - Municipal Historic Site, 8 tombs ** {{ill|Shichishiyama Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:七石山横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Sakae-ku, Yokohama|en}}[[ja:栄区]], Yokohama) - Over 100 tombs originally ** {{ill|Miyanomae Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:宮ノ前横穴墓群]] (Sakae-ku, Yokohama) - 23 tombs ** {{ill|Hikoshigaoka Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:ひこしヶ谷横穴墓群]] (Sakae-ku, Yokohama) - 12 tombs
=== Tokai Region === * {{ill|Kashiya Cave Tombs|ja|柏谷横穴群|en}}[[ja:柏谷横穴群]] ({{ill|Kannami|en}}[[ja:函南町]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, 106 tombs * {{ill|Kitaema Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:北江間横穴群]] ({{ill|Izunokuni|en}}[[ja:伊豆の国市]], Shizuoka) - National Historic Site, 101 tombs * {{ill|Eura Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:江浦横穴群]] ({{ill|Numazu|en}}[[ja:沼津市]], Shizuoka) - Prefectural Historic Site, 92 tombs
=== Hokuriku Region === * {{ill|Hōōzan Yokoguchi Kofun|en}}[[ja:法皇山横穴古墳]] ({{ill|Kaga, Ishikawa|en}}[[ja:加賀市]], [[Ishikawa Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, over 150 tombs
=== Kinki Region === * {{ill|Takaida Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:高井田横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|en}}[[ja:柏原市]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, over 200 tombs * {{ill|Anfukuji Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:安福寺横穴群]] (Kashiwara, Osaka) - 32 tombs
=== Chūgoku Region === * {{ill|Jūōmen Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:十王免横穴群]] ({{ill|Matsue|en}}[[ja:松江市]], [[Shimane Prefecture]]) - 37 tombs * {{ill|Kitsunedani Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:狐谷横穴墓群]] (Matsue, Shimane) - 16 tombs * {{ill|Kamikonishi Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:上塩冶横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Izumo, Shimane|en}}[[ja:出雲市]], Shimane) * {{ill|Takatsuku Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:高津久横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Chibu, Shimane|en}}[[ja:知夫村]], [[Oki District]])
=== Kyushu Region === * {{ill|Takenami Site|en}}[[ja:竹並遺跡]] ({{ill|Yukuhashi|en}}[[ja:行橋市]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]) - Over 1,000 tombs * {{ill|Shiroyama Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:城山横穴群]] ({{ill|Fukuchi, Fukuoka|en}}[[ja:福智町]], Fukuoka) - National Historic Site, over 200 tombs * {{ill|Mizumachi Site|en}}[[ja:水町遺跡]] ({{ill|Nōgata|en}}[[ja:直方市]], Fukuoka) - Prefectural Historic Site, over 40 tombs * {{ill|Kozuki Yokoguchi|en}}[[ja:古月横穴]] ({{ill|Kurate, Fukuoka|en}}[[ja:鞍手町]], Fukuoka) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Ishinuki Nagino Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:石貫ナギノ横穴群]] ({{ill|Tamana|en}}[[ja:玉名市]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Ishinuki Anakanon Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:石貫穴観音横穴群]] (Tamana, Kumamoto) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Nabeta Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:鍋田横穴群]] ({{ill|Yamaga, Kumamoto|en}}[[ja:山鹿市]], Kumamoto) - National Historic Site, 60 tombs * {{ill|Ōmura Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:大村横穴群]] ({{ill|Hitoyoshi|en}}[[ja:人吉市]], Kumamoto) - National Historic Site, 27 tombs * {{ill|Yokkaichi Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:四日市横穴群]] ({{ill|Usa|en}}[[ja:宇佐市]], [[Ōita Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, 161 tombs * {{ill|Takio Hyakka Yokoguchi Kofun Cluster|en}}[[ja:滝尾百穴横穴古墳群]] ({{ill|Ōita, Ōita|en}}[[ja:大分市]], Ōita) - Municipal Historic Site, 75 tombs * {{ill|Hasunogaike Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:蓮ヶ池横穴群]] ({{ill|Miyazaki, Miyazaki|en}}[[ja:宮崎市]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site

'''Yokohama Tombs''' (横穴墓, Yokōbo/Ouketsubo<ref name="rekihaku">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=https://www.rekihaku.city.yokohama.jp/maibun/qa/detail.php?seq=22|title=Q：横穴墓ってなんて読むのですか?|publisher=Yokohama City Foundation for History and Culture|accessdate=2018-07-28]]</ref>) are burial tombs generally carved into slopes or cliffs, typically measuring around 2 meters in height and extending several meters in depth. == Characteristics == [[File:市ヶ尾横穴墓群B群.JPG|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Ichigao Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:市ヶ尾横穴墓群]] B Cluster: Forecourt and entrance.]] [[File:出山横穴墓群 2009.03.21 - panoramio (5).jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Izuyama Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:出山横穴墓群]]: Interior view of the burial chamber.]] [[File:長柄横穴群11号墓棺座.JPG|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Nagara Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:長柄横穴群]] Tomb 11: Stone coffin base.]] Yokohama tombs closely resemble [[kofun]] tombs with horizontal stone chambers. These tombs typically lack burial mounds but include exceptions. Burial chambers often feature bases for coffins (棺座, kanza) and showcase various ceiling styles, such as house-shaped, dome-shaped, or arch-shaped. By the early 7th century, they had reached the Hokuriku, Kanto, and southern Tohoku regions. The practice peaked around the time of the {{ill|Thin Burial Edict|en}}[[ja:薄葬令]] before declining in the late 8th century.<ref name="#1"/>

== Distribution and Notable Yokoguchi Tombs == These tombs are found from Kyushu through San'in, Kinki, Hokuriku, and Tokai, with a high concentration in [[Southern Kantō]]. The northern limit is thought to be in northern [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. Approximately 3,000 examples exist in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]].

{{distinguish|Yokokutsu-shiki sekishitsu]] [[File:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群11号墓.jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Yōkokuji Valley Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群]] Tomb 11: House-shaped chamber.]] [[File:Yoshimi Yoshimi-hyakuana 2.JPG|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Yoshimi Hundred Caves|en}}[[ja:吉見百穴]] (November 2010)]] [[File:柏谷横穴群.JPG|right|250px|thumb|{{ill|Kashiya Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:柏谷横穴群]] (February 2013)]]
'''Yokohama Tombs''' (横穴墓, Yokōbo/Ouketsubo<ref name="rekihaku">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=https://www.rekihaku.city.yokohama.jp/maibun/qa/detail.php?seq=22|title=Q：横穴墓ってなんて読むのですか?|publisher=Yokohama City Foundation for History and Culture|accessdate=2018-07-28]]</ref>) are burial tombs generally carved into slopes or cliffs, typically measuring around 2 meters in height and extending several meters in depth. These tombs served as burial chambers in ancient East Asia and are most commonly associated with the [[Kofun period]] in Japan, where this burial type emerged and became widely used.
== Characteristics == [[File:市ヶ尾横穴墓群B群.JPG|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Ichigao Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:市ヶ尾横穴墓群]] B Cluster: Forecourt and entrance.]] [[File:出山横穴墓群 2009.03.21 - panoramio (5).jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Deyama Graves|ja|出山横穴墓群|en}}[[ja:出山横穴墓群]]: Interior view of the burial chamber.]] [[File:長柄横穴群11号墓棺座.JPG|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Nagara Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:長柄横穴群]] Tomb 11: Stone coffin base.]] Yokohama tombs closely resemble [[kofun]] tombs with horizontal stone chambers. They were carved into rock faces, featuring entrances leading to passageways (羨道, sendō), which open into burial chambers (玄室, genshitsu). These tombs typically lack burial mounds but include exceptions. Burial chambers often feature bases for coffins (棺座, kanza) and showcase various ceiling styles, such as house-shaped, dome-shaped, or arch-shaped. Some tombs incorporate forecourts and intermediate rooms between the passageway and chamber.<ref name="#1">{{Cite book|language=ja|author=Keiji Nagahara|editor=Eiichi Ishigami|title=Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1999|isbn=4-00-080093-0]]</ref>
In {{ill|Kamakura|en}}[[ja:鎌倉市]], Yokohama tombs feature the distinctive "Kamakura-type" (鎌倉型横穴墓), where burial shelves or smaller inner chambers were carved into the rear walls of burial chambers.<ref name="#2">{{Cite book|language=ja|author=Sakae Ward Historical Committee|title=The History of Sakae|publisher=Sakae Ward Community Promotion Division|year=2013]]</ref>
Yokohama tombs are typically found in clusters (横穴墓群, yokohama bokun). The {{ill|Yoshimi Hundred Caves|en}}[[ja:吉見百穴]] in {{ill|Yoshimi|en}}[[ja:吉見町]], {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en}}[[ja:埼玉県]], comprises over 200 tombs and is a prominent example designated as a National Historic Site.
Some tombs also feature wall carvings and paintings, often categorized as decorative kofun. Colored embellishments have been discovered in regions such as [[Kyushu]], [[Kanto]], and parts of [[Tohoku]].
== Origins and Evolution == Yokohama tombs originated in northern Kyushu around the late 5th century during the Kofun period and subsequently spread across western Japan, including the San'in, Sanyo, Kinki, and Tokai regions by the mid-6th century. By the early 7th century, they had reached the Hokuriku, Kanto, and southern Tohoku regions. The practice peaked around the time of the {{ill|Thin Burial Edict|en}}[[ja:薄葬令]] before declining in the late 8th century.<ref name="#1"/>
== Name == Yokohama tombs are sometimes called "yokoana kofun" (横穴古墳), but technically the term "kofun" refers to burial mounds, which these tombs lack. They are often grouped under broader definitions of kofun but differ significantly in their construction and purpose.<ref name="rekihaku"/>
The term "yokohama tombs" became common in the 1960s, gaining popularity among researchers in eastern Japan, whereas western Japan researchers traditionally referred to them as "yokoana." The term "yokohama" is preferred for consistency.<ref name="rekihaku"/>
== Distribution and Notable Yokoguchi Tombs == These tombs are found from Kyushu through San'in, Kinki, Hokuriku, and Tokai, with a high concentration in [[Southern Kantō]]. The northern limit is thought to be in northern [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. Approximately 3,000 examples exist in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]].

[[File:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群11号墓.jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Yōkokuji Valley Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群]] Tomb 11: House-shaped chamber.]] [[File:Yoshimi Yoshimi-hyakuana 2.JPG|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Yoshimi Hundred Caves|en}}[[ja:吉見百穴]] (November 2010)]] [[File:柏谷横穴群.JPG|right|250px|thumb|{{ill|Kashiya Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:柏谷横穴群]] (February 2013)]]
'''Yokohama Tombs''' (横穴墓, Yokōbo/Ouketsubo<ref name="rekihaku">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=https://www.rekihaku.city.yokohama.jp/maibun/qa/detail.php?seq=22|title=Q：横穴墓ってなんて読むのですか?|publisher=Yokohama City Foundation for History and Culture|accessdate=2018-07-28]]</ref>) are burial tombs generally carved into slopes or cliffs, typically measuring around 2 meters in height and extending several meters in depth. These tombs served as burial chambers in ancient East Asia and are most commonly associated with the [[Kofun period]] in Japan, where this burial type emerged and became widely used.
[[ja:市ヶ尾横穴墓群]] [[ja:出山横穴墓群]] [[ja:長柄横穴群]] [[ja:鎌倉市]] [[ja:吉見百穴]] [[ja:吉見町]] [[ja:埼玉県]] [[ja:薄葬令]]
== Distribution and Notable Yokoguchi Tombs == These tombs are found from Kyushu through San'in, Kinki, Hokuriku, and Tokai, with a high concentration in [[Southern Kantō]]. The northern limit is thought to be in northern [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. Approximately 3,000 examples exist in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]].
=== Tōhoku Region === * {{ill|Yamahata Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:山畑横穴群]] ({{ill|Ōsaki, Miyagi|en}}[[ja:大崎市]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, 26 tombs * {{ill|Atagoyama Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:愛宕山横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Taihaku-ku, Sendai|en}}[[ja:太白区]], {{ill|Sendai|en}}[[ja:仙台市]]) * {{ill|Mogasaki Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:茂ヶ崎横穴墓群]] (Taihaku-ku, Sendai) - 25 tombs, no longer extant * {{ill|Sōzenji Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:宗禅寺横穴墓群]] (Taihaku-ku, Sendai) * {{ill|Kiyotosaku Yokoguchi|en}}[[ja:清戸迫横穴墓]] ({{ill|Futaba, Fukushima|en}}[[ja:双葉町]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, over 300 tombs * {{ill|Izumizaki Yokoguchi|en}}[[ja:泉崎横穴墓]] ({{ill|Nishishirakawa District|en}}[[ja:西白河郡]], {{ill|Izumizaki, Fukushima|en}}[[ja:泉崎村]]) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Nakata Cave Tombs|en}}[[ja:中田横穴]] ({{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|en}}[[ja:いわき市]]) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Hayama Cave Tomb|en}}[[ja:羽山横穴]] ({{ill|Minamisōma|en}}[[ja:南相馬市]], Fukushima Prefecture) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Wada Daibutsu and Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:和田大仏及び横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Sukagawa|en}}[[ja:須賀川市]], Fukushima Prefecture) - Municipal Historic Site

[[File:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群11号墓.jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Yōkokuji Valley Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群]] Tomb 11: House-shaped chamber.]] [[File:Yoshimi Yoshimi-hyakuana 2.JPG|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Yoshimi Hundred Caves|en}}[[ja:吉見百穴]] (November 2010)]] [[File:柏谷横穴群.JPG|right|250px|thumb|{{ill|Kashiya Cave Tombs|en}}[[ja:柏谷横穴群]] (February 2013)]]
'''Yokohama Tombs''' (横穴墓, Yokōbo/Ouketsubo<ref name="rekihaku">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=https://www.rekihaku.city.yokohama.jp/maibun/qa/detail.php?seq=22|title=Q：横穴墓ってなんて読むのですか?|publisher=Yokohama City Foundation for History and Culture|accessdate=2018-07-28]]</ref>) are burial tombs generally carved into slopes or cliffs, typically measuring around 2 meters in height and extending several meters in depth. These tombs served as burial chambers in ancient East Asia and are most commonly associated with the [[Kofun period]] in Japan, where this burial type emerged and became widely used.
[[ja:市ヶ尾横穴墓群]] [[ja:出山横穴墓群]] [[ja:長柄横穴群]] [[ja:鎌倉市]] [[ja:吉見百穴]] [[ja:吉見町]] [[ja:埼玉県]] [[ja:薄葬令]]
== Distribution and Notable Yokoguchi Tombs == These tombs are found from Kyushu through San'in, Kinki, Hokuriku, and Tokai, with a high concentration in [[Southern Kantō]]. The northern limit is thought to be in northern [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. Approximately 3,000 examples exist in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]].
=== Tōhoku Region === * {{ill|Yamahata Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:山畑横穴群]] ({{ill|Ōsaki, Miyagi|en}}[[ja:大崎市]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, 26 tombs * {{ill|Atagoyama Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:愛宕山横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Taihaku-ku, Sendai|en}}[[ja:太白区]], {{ill|Sendai|en}}[[ja:仙台市]]) * {{ill|Mogasaki Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:茂ヶ崎横穴墓群]] (Taihaku-ku, Sendai) - 25 tombs, no longer extant * {{ill|Sōzenji Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:宗禅寺横穴墓群]] (Taihaku-ku, Sendai) * {{ill|Kiyotosaku Yokoguchi|en}}[[ja:清戸迫横穴墓]] ({{ill|Futaba, Fukushima|en}}[[ja:双葉町]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, over 300 tombs * {{ill|Izumizaki Yokoguchi|en}}[[ja:泉崎横穴墓]] ({{ill|Nishishirakawa District|en}}[[ja:西白河郡]], {{ill|Izumizaki, Fukushima|en}}[[ja:泉崎村]]) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Nakata Cave Tombs|en}}[[ja:中田横穴]] ({{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|en}}[[ja:いわき市]]) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Hayama Cave Tomb|en}}[[ja:羽山横穴]] ({{ill|Minamisōma|en}}[[ja:南相馬市]], Fukushima Prefecture) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Wada Daibutsu and Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:和田大仏及び横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Sukagawa|en}}[[ja:須賀川市]], Fukushima Prefecture) - Municipal Historic Site

[[File:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群11号墓.jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Yōkokuji Valley Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群]] Tomb 11: House-shaped chamber.]] [[ja:吉見百穴]] [[ja:柏谷横穴群]] '''Yokohama Tombs''' (横穴墓, Yokōbo/Ouketsubo<ref name="rekihaku">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=https://www.rekihaku.city.yokohama.jp/maibun/qa/detail.php?seq=22|title=Q：横穴墓ってなんて読むのですか?|publisher=Yokohama City Foundation for History and Culture|accessdate=2018-07-28]]</ref>) are burial tombs generally carved into slopes or cliffs, typically measuring around 2 meters in height and extending several meters in depth. These tombs served as burial chambers in ancient East Asia and are most commonly associated with the [[Kofun period]] in Japan, where this burial type emerged and became widely used. '''Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Tomb Group''' (楊谷寺谷戸横穴群), also known as '''Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group''' (楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群) or '''Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Burial Group''', is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Ōiso, Naka District, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It has been designated as a Kanagawa Prefecture Historic Site.
== Overview == The tomb group is situated on the southern slope of the ridges in the [[Ōiso Hills]], an area known for having one of Japan’s highest concentrations of rock-cut tombs. Located in a tributary valley of [[Koma Mountain]], 27 tombs have been identified, spread across four tiers (seven of which have been buried by soil deposition){{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}<ref name="Explanation Board Kanagawa"/>. Investigations were conducted in [[1879]] (Meiji 12) and [[1962]] (Shōwa 37).
This burial group features a variety of architectural styles, beginning with tomb 11, which has a square layout with a gabled-roof design. This evolved into tomb 5, which has a dome-shaped ceiling—a simplified version of the hip-and-gable roof. Further simplifications resulted in the arch-shaped ceilings of tombs 4 and 7<ref name="Explanation Board Kanagawa">Historic Site Explanation Board (Kanagawa Prefecture Board of Education, 1982).</ref>. Due to looting, only fragmented grave goods remain{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}. As one of the most well-known and representative burial groups in the Ōiso Hills, it is considered significant for studying the structural evolution of rock-cut tombs{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}<ref name="Explanation Board Ōiso Town">Historic Site Explanation Board (Ōiso Town Board of Education, 2010).</ref>.
In [[1966]] (Shōwa 41), the site was designated a Historic Site by Kanagawa Prefecture.
== Excavation History == * [[1879]] (Meiji 12): Investigated by [[Tsuboi Shōgorō]]{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group (Kofun)|1989}}. * [[1962]] (Shōwa 37): Survey conducted by [[Akaboshi Naotada]] and the Ōiso Town Board of Education, with a report published in [[1964]]{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group (Kofun)|1989}}. * [[1966]] (Shōwa 41), July 19: Designated as a Kanagawa Prefecture Historic Site<ref name="Cultural Property List"/>.

== Distribution and Notable Yokoguchi Tombs == These tombs are found from Kyushu through San'in, Kinki, Hokuriku, and Tokai, with a high concentration in [[Southern Kantō]]. The northern limit is thought to be in northern [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. Approximately 3,000 examples exist in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]].

'''Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Tomb Group''' (楊谷寺谷戸横穴群), also known as '''Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group''' (楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群) or '''Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Burial Group''', is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Ōiso, Naka District, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It has been designated as a Kanagawa Prefecture Historic Site.[[File:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群11号墓.jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Yōkokuji Valley Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群]] Tomb 11: House-shaped chamber.]] [[ja:吉見百穴]] [[ja:柏谷横穴群]] '''Yokohama Tombs''' (横穴墓, Yokōbo/Ouketsubo<ref name="rekihaku">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=https://www.rekihaku.city.yokohama.jp/maibun/qa/detail.php?seq=22|title=Q：横穴墓ってなんて読むのですか?|publisher=Yokohama City Foundation for History and Culture|accessdate=2018-07-28]]</ref>) are burial tombs generally carved into slopes or cliffs, typically measuring around 2 meters in height and extending several meters in depth. These tombs served as burial chambers in ancient East Asia and are most commonly associated with the [[Kofun period]] in Japan, where this burial type emerged and became widely used.
== Overview == The tomb group is situated on the southern slope of the ridges in the [[Ōiso Hills]], an area known for having one of Japan’s highest concentrations of rock-cut tombs. Located in a tributary valley of [[Koma Mountain]], 27 tombs have been identified, spread across four tiers (seven of which have been buried by soil deposition){{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}<ref name="Explanation Board Kanagawa"/>. Investigations were conducted in [[1879]] (Meiji 12) and [[1962]] (Shōwa 37).
This burial group features a variety of architectural styles, beginning with tomb 11, which has a square layout with a gabled-roof design. This evolved into tomb 5, which has a dome-shaped ceiling—a simplified version of the hip-and-gable roof. Further simplifications resulted in the arch-shaped ceilings of tombs 4 and 7<ref name="Explanation Board Kanagawa">Historic Site Explanation Board (Kanagawa Prefecture Board of Education, 1982).</ref>. Due to looting, only fragmented grave goods remain{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}. As one of the most well-known and representative burial groups in the Ōiso Hills, it is considered significant for studying the structural evolution of rock-cut tombs{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}<ref name="Explanation Board Ōiso Town">Historic Site Explanation Board (Ōiso Town Board of Education, 2010).</ref>.
In [[1966]] (Shōwa 41), the site was designated a Historic Site by Kanagawa Prefecture.
== Excavation History == * [[1879]] (Meiji 12): Investigated by [[Tsuboi Shōgorō]]{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group (Kofun)|1989}}. * [[1962]] (Shōwa 37): Survey conducted by [[Akaboshi Naotada]] and the Ōiso Town Board of Education, with a report published in [[1964]]{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group (Kofun)|1989}}. * [[1966]] (Shōwa 41), July 19: Designated as a Kanagawa Prefecture Historic Site<ref name="Cultural Property List"/>.

== Distribution and Notable Yokoguchi Tombs == These tombs are found from Kyushu through San'in, Kinki, Hokuriku, and Tokai, with a high concentration in [[Southern Kantō]]. The northern limit is thought to be in northern [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. Approximately 3,000 examples exist in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]].

Further simplifications resulted in the arch-shaped ceilings of tombs 4 and 7<ref name="Explanation Board Kanagawa">Historic Site Explanation Board (Kanagawa Prefecture Board of Education, 1982).</ref>. Due to looting, only fragmented grave goods remain{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}. As one of the most well-known and representative burial groups in the Ōiso Hills, it is considered significant for studying the structural evolution of rock-cut tombs{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}<ref name="Explanation Board Ōiso Town">Historic Site Explanation Board (Ōiso Town Board of Education, 2010).</ref>.
In [[1966]] (Shōwa 41), the site was designated a Historic Site by Kanagawa Prefecture.
== Excavation History == * [[1879]] (Meiji 12): Investigated by [[Tsuboi Shōgorō]]{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group (Kofun)|1989}}. * [[1962]] (Shōwa 37): Survey conducted by [[Akaboshi Naotada]] and the Ōiso Town Board of Education, with a report published in [[1964]]{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group (Kofun)|1989}}. * [[1966]] (Shōwa 41), July 19: Designated as a Kanagawa Prefecture Historic Site<ref name="Cultural Property List"/>.

== Distribution and Notable Yokoguchi Tombs == These tombs are found from Kyushu through San'in, Kinki, Hokuriku, and Tokai, with a high concentration in [[Southern Kantō]]. The northern limit is thought to be in northern [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. Approximately 3,000 examples exist in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]].

'''Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Tomb Group''' (楊谷寺谷戸横穴群), also known as '''Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group''' (楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群) or '''Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Burial Group''', is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Ōiso, Naka District, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It has been designated as a Kanagawa Prefecture Historic Site.[[File:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群11号墓.jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Yōkokuji Valley Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群]] Tomb 11: House-shaped chamber.]] [[ja:吉見百穴]] [[ja:柏谷横穴群]] '''Yokohama Tombs''' (横穴墓, Yokōbo/Ouketsubo<ref name="rekihaku">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=https://www.rekihaku.city.yokohama.jp/maibun/qa/detail.php?seq=22|title=Q：横穴墓ってなんて読むのですか?|publisher=Yokohama City Foundation for History and Culture|accessdate=2018-07-28]]</ref>) are burial tombs generally carved into slopes or cliffs, typically measuring around 2 meters in height and extending several meters in depth. These tombs served as burial chambers in ancient East Asia and are most commonly associated with the [[Kofun period]] in Japan, where this burial type emerged and became widely used.
== Overview == The tomb group is situated on the southern slope of the ridges in the [[Ōiso Hills]], an area known for having one of Japan’s highest concentrations of rock-cut tombs. Located in a tributary valley of [[Koma Mountain]], 27 tombs have been identified, spread across four tiers (seven of which have been buried by soil deposition){{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}<ref name="Explanation Board Kanagawa"/>. Investigations were conducted in [[1879]] (Meiji 12) and [[1962]] (Shōwa 37).
This burial group features a variety of architectural styles, beginning with tomb 11, which has a square layout with a gabled-roof design. This evolved into tomb 5, which has a dome-shaped ceiling—a simplified version of the hip-and-gable roof. Further simplifications resulted in the arch-shaped ceilings of tombs 4 and 7<ref name="Explanation Board Kanagawa">Historic Site Explanation Board (Kanagawa Prefecture Board of Education, 1982).</ref>. Due to looting, only fragmented grave goods remain{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}. As one of the most well-known and representative burial groups in the Ōiso Hills, it is considered significant for studying the structural evolution of rock-cut tombs{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}<ref name="Explanation Board Ōiso Town">Historic Site Explanation Board (Ōiso Town Board of Education, 2010).</ref>.
In [[1966]] (Shōwa 41), the site was designated a Historic Site by Kanagawa Prefecture.
== Excavation History == * [[1879]] (Meiji 12): Investigated by [[Tsuboi Shōgorō]]{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group (Kofun)|1989}}. * [[1962]] (Shōwa 37): Survey conducted by [[Akaboshi Naotada]] and the Ōiso Town Board of Education, with a report published in [[1964]]{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group (Kofun)|1989}}. * [[1966]] (Shōwa 41), July 19: Designated as a Kanagawa Prefecture Historic Site<ref name="Cultural Property List"/>.

== Distribution and Notable Yokoguchi Tombs == These tombs are found from Kyushu through San'in, Kinki, Hokuriku, and Tokai, with a high concentration in [[Southern Kantō]]. The northern limit is thought to be in northern [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. Approximately 3,000 examples exist in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]].

'''Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Tomb Group''' (楊谷寺谷戸横穴群), also known as '''Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group''' (楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群) or '''Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Burial Group''', is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Ōiso, Naka District, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It has been designated as a Kanagawa Prefecture Historic Site.[[File:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群11号墓.jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Yōkokuji Valley Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群]] Tomb 11: House-shaped chamber.]] [[ja:吉見百穴]] [[ja:柏谷横穴群]] '''Yokohama Tombs''' (横穴墓, Yokōbo/Ouketsubo<ref name="rekihaku">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=https://www.rekihaku.city.yokohama.jp/maibun/qa/detail.php?seq=22|title=Q：横穴墓ってなんて読むのですか?|publisher=Yokohama City Foundation for History and Culture|accessdate=2018-07-28]]</ref>) are burial tombs generally carved into slopes or cliffs, typically measuring around 2 meters in height and extending several meters in depth. These tombs served as burial chambers in ancient East Asia and are most commonly associated with the [[Kofun period]] in Japan, where this burial type emerged and became widely used.
== Overview == The tomb group is situated on the southern slope of the ridges in the [[Ōiso Hills]], an area known for having one of Japan’s highest concentrations of rock-cut tombs. Located in a tributary valley of [[Koma Mountain]], 27 tombs have been identified, spread across four tiers (seven of which have been buried by soil deposition){{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}<ref name="Explanation Board Kanagawa"/>. Investigations were conducted in [[1879]] (Meiji 12) and [[1962]] (Shōwa 37).
This burial group features a variety of architectural styles, beginning with tomb 11, which has a square layout with a gabled-roof design. This evolved into tomb 5, which has a dome-shaped ceiling—a simplified version of the hip-and-gable roof. Further simplifications resulted in the arch-shaped ceilings of tombs 4 and 7<ref name="Explanation Board Kanagawa">Historic Site Explanation Board (Kanagawa Prefecture Board of Education, 1982).</ref>. Due to looting, only fragmented grave goods remain{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}. As one of the most well-known and representative burial groups in the Ōiso Hills, it is considered significant for studying the structural evolution of rock-cut tombs{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}<ref name="Explanation Board Ōiso Town">Historic Site Explanation Board (Ōiso Town Board of Education, 2010).</ref>.
In [[1966]] (Shōwa 41), the site was designated a Historic Site by Kanagawa Prefecture.
== Excavation History == * [[1879]] (Meiji 12): Investigated by [[Tsuboi Shōgorō]]{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group (Kofun)|1989}}. * [[1962]] (Shōwa 37): Survey conducted by [[Akaboshi Naotada]] and the Ōiso Town Board of Education, with a report published in [[1964]]{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group (Kofun)|1989}}. * [[1966]] (Shōwa 41), July 19: Designated as a Kanagawa Prefecture Historic Site<ref name="Cultural Property List"/>.

[[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese burial practices]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Corridor-type kofun]] [[Category:kkkkk]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:kk]] [[ja:横穴墓]] [[Category:Corridor-type kofun]] [[Category:kkkkk]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Corridor-type kofun forks]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:kk]] {{Kofun navbar}}[[Category:kk]] [[Category:fixing the kk issue]]

'''Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Tomb Group''' (楊谷寺谷戸横穴群), also known as '''Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group''' (楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群) or '''Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Burial Group''', is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Ōiso, Naka District, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It has been designated as a Kanagawa Prefecture Historic Site.[[File:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群11号墓.jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Yōkokuji Valley Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群]] Tomb 11: House-shaped chamber.]] [[ja:吉見百穴]] [[ja:柏谷横穴群]] '''Yokohama Tombs''' (横穴墓, Yokōbo/Ouketsubo<ref name="rekihaku">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=https://www.rekihaku.city.yokohama.jp/maibun/qa/detail.php?seq=22|title=Q：横穴墓ってなんて読むのですか?|publisher=Yokohama City Foundation for History and Culture|accessdate=2018-07-28]]</ref>) are burial tombs generally carved into slopes or cliffs, typically measuring around 2 meters in height and extending several meters in depth. These tombs served as burial chambers in ancient East Asia and are most commonly associated with the [[Kofun period]] in Japan, where this burial type emerged and became widely used.
== Overview == The tomb group is situated on the southern slope of the ridges in the [[Ōiso Hills]], an area known for having one of Japan’s highest concentrations of rock-cut tombs. Located in a tributary valley of [[Koma Mountain]], 27 tombs have been identified, spread across four tiers (seven of which have been buried by soil deposition){{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}<ref name="Explanation Board Kanagawa"/>. Investigations were conducted in [[1879]] (Meiji 12) and [[1962]] (Shōwa 37).
This burial group features a variety of architectural styles, beginning with tomb 11, which has a square layout with a gabled-roof design. This evolved into tomb 5, which has a dome-shaped ceiling—a simplified version of the hip-and-gable roof. Further simplifications resulted in the arch-shaped ceilings of tombs 4 and 7<ref name="Explanation Board Kanagawa">Historic Site Explanation Board (Kanagawa Prefecture Board of Education, 1982).</ref>. Due to looting, only fragmented grave goods remain{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}. As one of the most well-known and representative burial groups in the Ōiso Hills, it is considered significant for studying the structural evolution of rock-cut tombs{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}<ref name="Explanation Board Ōiso Town">Historic Site Explanation Board (Ōiso Town Board of Education, 2010).</ref>.
In [[1966]] (Shōwa 41), the site was designated a Historic Site by Kanagawa Prefecture.
== Excavation History == * [[1879]] (Meiji 12): Investigated by [[Tsuboi Shōgorō]]{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group (Kofun)|1989}}. * [[1962]] (Shōwa 37): Survey conducted by [[Akaboshi Naotada]] and the Ōiso Town Board of Education, with a report published in [[1964]]{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group (Kofun)|1989}}. * [[1966]] (Shōwa 41), July 19: Designated as a Kanagawa Prefecture Historic Site<ref name="Cultural Property List"/>.
== Major Rock-Cut Tombs == {| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;white-space:nowrap;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |- ! Tomb !! Entrance !! Interior |- |'''Tomb 11''' |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-11-1.jpg|200px]] |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-11-2.jpg|200px]] |- |'''Tomb 5''' |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-5-1.jpg|200px]] |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-5-2.jpg|200px]] |- |'''Tomb 4''' |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-4-1.jpg|200px]] |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-4-2.jpg|200px]] |- |'''Tomb 7''' |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-7-1.jpg|200px]] |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-7-2.jpg|200px]] |- |}
== Cultural Property == === Kanagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === * Historic Site ** '''Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Tomb Group''' - Designated on July 19, [[1966]] (Shōwa 41)<ref name="Cultural Property List">{{PDFlink|[http://www.town.oiso.kanagawa.jp/material/files/group/30/bunkazai21218.pdf Designated Cultural Properties List]}} (Ōiso Town Website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Ōiso Town Local History Museum''' (Nishi-Oiso, Ōiso Town, within the Kanagawa Prefectural Ōiso Shiroyama Park) - Stores and displays artifacts from Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group.

== Distribution and Notable Yokoguchi Tombs == These tombs are found from Kyushu through San'in, Kinki, Hokuriku, and Tokai, with a high concentration in [[Southern Kantō]]. The northern limit is thought to be in northern [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. Approximately 3,000 examples exist in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]].

revision.text:
'''Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Tomb Group''' (楊谷寺谷戸横穴群), also known as '''Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group''' (楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群) or '''Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Burial Group''', is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Ōiso, Naka District, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It has been designated as a Kanagawa Prefecture Historic Site.[[File:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群11号墓.jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Yōkokuji Valley Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群]] Tomb 11: House-shaped chamber.]] [[ja:吉見百穴]] [[ja:柏谷横穴群]] '''Yokohama Tombs''' (横穴墓, Yokōbo/Ouketsubo<ref name="rekihaku">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=https://www.rekihaku.city.yokohama.jp/maibun/qa/detail.php?seq=22|title=Q：横穴墓ってなんて読むのですか?|publisher=Yokohama City Foundation for History and Culture|accessdate=2018-07-28]]</ref>) are burial tombs generally carved into slopes or cliffs, typically measuring around 2 meters in height and extending several meters in depth. These tombs served as burial chambers in ancient East Asia and are most commonly associated with the [[Kofun period]] in Japan, where this burial type emerged and became widely used.
== Overview == The tomb group is situated on the southern slope of the ridges in the [[Ōiso Hills]], an area known for having one of Japan’s highest concentrations of rock-cut tombs. Located in a tributary valley of [[Koma Mountain]], 27 tombs have been identified, spread across four tiers (seven of which have been buried by soil deposition){{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}<ref name="Explanation Board Kanagawa"/>. Investigations were conducted in [[1879]] (Meiji 12) and [[1962]] (Shōwa 37).
This burial group features a variety of architectural styles, beginning with tomb 11, which has a square layout with a gabled-roof design. This evolved into tomb 5, which has a dome-shaped ceiling—a simplified version of the hip-and-gable roof. Further simplifications resulted in the arch-shaped ceilings of tombs 4 and 7<ref name="Explanation Board Kanagawa">Historic Site Explanation Board (Kanagawa Prefecture Board of Education, 1982).</ref>. Due to looting, only fragmented grave goods remain{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}. As one of the most well-known and representative burial groups in the Ōiso Hills, it is considered significant for studying the structural evolution of rock-cut tombs{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}<ref name="Explanation Board Ōiso Town">Historic Site Explanation Board (Ōiso Town Board of Education, 2010).</ref>.
In [[1966]] (Shōwa 41), the site was designated a Historic Site by Kanagawa Prefecture.
== Excavation History == * [[1879]] (Meiji 12): Investigated by [[Tsuboi Shōgorō]]{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group (Kofun)|1989}}. * [[1962]] (Shōwa 37): Survey conducted by [[Akaboshi Naotada]] and the Ōiso Town Board of Education, with a report published in [[1964]]{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group (Kofun)|1989}}. * [[1966]] (Shōwa 41), July 19: Designated as a Kanagawa Prefecture Historic Site<ref name="Cultural Property List"/>.
== Major Rock-Cut Tombs == {| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;white-space:nowrap;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |- ! Tomb !! Entrance !! Interior |- |'''Tomb 11''' |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-11-1.jpg|200px]] |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-11-2.jpg|200px]] |- |'''Tomb 5''' |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-5-1.jpg|200px]] |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-5-2.jpg|200px]] |- |'''Tomb 4''' |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-4-1.jpg|200px]] |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-4-2.jpg|200px]] |- |'''Tomb 7''' |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-7-1.jpg|200px]] |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-7-2.jpg|200px]] |- |}
== Cultural Property == === Kanagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === * Historic Site ** '''Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Tomb Group''' - Designated on July 19, [[1966]] (Shōwa 41)<ref name="Cultural Property List">{{PDFlink|[http://www.town.oiso.kanagawa.jp/material/files/group/30/bunkazai21218.pdf Designated Cultural Properties List]}} (Ōiso Town Website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Ōiso Town Local History Museum''' (Nishi-Oiso, Ōiso Town, within the Kanagawa Prefectural Ōiso Shiroyama Park) - Stores and displays artifacts from Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group.
== References == {{reflist}}

[[ja:市ヶ尾横穴墓群]] [[ja:出山横穴墓群]] [[ja:長柄横穴群]] [[ja:鎌倉市]] [[ja:吉見百穴]] [[ja:吉見町]] [[ja:埼玉県]] [[ja:薄葬令]]
== Distribution and Notable Yokoguchi Tombs == These tombs are found from Kyushu through San'in, Kinki, Hokuriku, and Tokai, with a high concentration in [[Southern Kantō]]. The northern limit is thought to be in northern [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. Approximately 3,000 examples exist in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]].
=== Tōhoku Region === * {{ill|Yamahata Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:山畑横穴群]] ({{ill|Ōsaki, Miyagi|en}}[[ja:大崎市]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, 26 tombs * {{ill|Atagoyama Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:愛宕山横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Taihaku-ku, Sendai|en}}[[ja:太白区]], {{ill|Sendai|en}}[[ja:仙台市]]) * {{ill|Mogasaki Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:茂ヶ崎横穴墓群]] (Taihaku-ku, Sendai) - 25 tombs, no longer extant * {{ill|Sōzenji Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:宗禅寺横穴墓群]] (Taihaku-ku, Sendai) * {{ill|Kiyotosaku Yokoguchi|en}}[[ja:清戸迫横穴墓]] ({{ill|Futaba, Fukushima|en}}[[ja:双葉町]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, over 300 tombs * {{ill|Izumizaki Yokoguchi|en}}[[ja:泉崎横穴墓]] ({{ill|Nishishirakawa District|en}}[[ja:西白河郡]], {{ill|Izumizaki, Fukushima|en}}[[ja:泉崎村]]) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Nakata Cave Tombs|en}}[[ja:中田横穴]] ({{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|en}}[[ja:いわき市]]) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Hayama Cave Tomb|en}}[[ja:羽山横穴]] ({{ill|Minamisōma|en}}[[ja:南相馬市]], Fukushima Prefecture) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Wada Daibutsu and Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:和田大仏及び横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Sukagawa|en}}[[ja:須賀川市]], Fukushima Prefecture) - Municipal Historic Site
=== Kantō Region === * {{ill|Jūgorō-ana Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:十五郎穴横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Hitachinaka, Ibaraki|en}}[[ja:ひたちなか市]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]]) - Over 300 tombs * {{ill|Karanogosho Cave Tomb|en}}[[ja:唐御所横穴]] ({{ill|Nasukaragawa, Tochigi|en}}[[ja:那珂川町]], [[Tochigi Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, part of the Hokōda and Wami Yokoguchi Clusters * {{ill|Nagaoka Hyakka|en}}[[ja:長岡百穴古墳]] ({{ill|Utsunomiya|en}}[[ja:宇都宮市]], Tochigi Prefecture) - 52 tombs * {{ill|Yoshimi Hyakka|en}}[[ja:吉見百穴]] ({{ill|Yoshimi, Saitama|en}}[[ja:吉見町]], [[Saitama Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, over 200 tombs * {{ill|Kuroiwa Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:黒岩横穴墓群]] (Yoshimi, Saitama) - Over 30 tombs * {{ill|Jūrō Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:十郎横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Hatoyama, Saitama|en}}[[ja:鳩山町]], Saitama) - 3 tombs * {{ill|Takinojō Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:滝之城横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Tokorozawa|en}}[[ja:所沢市]], Saitama) - 9 tombs * {{ill|Kitaakitsu Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:北秋津横穴墓群]] (Tokorozawa, Saitama) - 8 tombs * {{ill|Bikuniyama Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:比丘尼山横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Higashimatsuyama|en}}[[ja:東松山市]], Saitama) - Over 50 tombs * {{ill|Takane Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:高根横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Kumagaya|en}}[[ja:熊谷市]], Saitama) - Municipal Historic Site * {{ill|Nagara Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:長柄横穴群]] ({{ill|Nagara, Chiba|en}}[[ja:長柄町]], [[Chiba Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, over 300 tombs * {{ill|Deyama Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:出山横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Mitaka, Tokyo|en}}[[ja:三鷹市]], [[Tokyo]]) - Metropolitan Historic Site, 10 tombs * {{ill|Nakawada Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:中和田横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Tama, Tokyo|en}}[[ja:多摩市]], Tokyo) - 14 tombs * {{ill|Todoroki Ravine Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:谷沢川横穴群]] ({{ill|Setagaya|en}}[[ja:世田谷区]], Tokyo) * {{ill|Ekoda Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:荏子田横穴]] ({{ill|Aoba-ku, Yokohama|en}}[[ja:青葉区]], {{ill|Yokohama|en}}[[ja:横浜市]], [[Kanagawa Prefecture]]) - Municipal Historic Site, 2 tombs * {{ill|Ichigao Yokoguchi Kofun Cluster|en}}[[ja:市ヶ尾横穴古墳群]] (Aoba-ku, Yokohama) - Prefectural Historic Site, 19 tombs * {{ill|Kashio River Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:㹨川流域横穴墓群]] (Kamakura-style tomb concentration) ** {{ill|Matsugasaki Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:松ヶ崎横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Kōnan-ku, Yokohama|en}}[[ja:港南区]], Yokohama) - Municipal Historic Site, 8 tombs ** {{ill|Shichisekiyama Cave Tomb Cluster|ja|七石山横穴墓群|en}}[[ja:七石山横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Sakae-ku, Yokohama|en}}[[ja:栄区]], Yokohama) - Over 100 tombs originally ** {{ill|Miyanomae Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:宮ノ前横穴墓群]] (Sakae-ku, Yokohama) - 23 tombs ** {{ill|Hikoshigaoka Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:ひこしヶ谷横穴墓群]] (Sakae-ku, Yokohama) - 12 tombs
=== Tokai Region === * {{ill|Kashiya Cave Tombs|ja|柏谷横穴群|en}}[[ja:柏谷横穴群]] ({{ill|Kannami|en}}[[ja:函南町]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, 106 tombs * {{ill|Kitaema Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:北江間横穴群]] ({{ill|Izunokuni|en}}[[ja:伊豆の国市]], Shizuoka) - National Historic Site, 101 tombs * {{ill|Eura Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:江浦横穴群]] ({{ill|Numazu|en}}[[ja:沼津市]], Shizuoka) - Prefectural Historic Site, 92 tombs
=== Hokuriku Region === * {{ill|Hōōzan Cave Tombs||ja|法皇山横穴古墳|en}}[[ja:法皇山横穴古墳]] ({{ill|Kaga, Ishikawa|en}}[[ja:加賀市]], [[Ishikawa Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, over 150 tombs
=== Kinki Region === * {{ill|Takaida Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:高井田横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|en}}[[ja:柏原市]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, over 200 tombs * {{ill|Anfukuji Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:安福寺横穴群]] (Kashiwara, Osaka) - 32 tombs
=== Chūgoku Region === * {{ill|Jūōmen Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:十王免横穴群]] ({{ill|Matsue|en}}[[ja:松江市]], [[Shimane Prefecture]]) - 37 tombs * {{ill|Kitsunedani Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:狐谷横穴墓群]] (Matsue, Shimane) - 16 tombs * {{ill|Kamikonishi Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:上塩冶横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Izumo, Shimane|en}}[[ja:出雲市]], Shimane) * {{ill|Takatsuku Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:高津久横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Chibu, Shimane|en}}[[ja:知夫村]], [[Oki District]])
=== Kyushu Region === * {{ill|Takenami Site|en}}[[ja:竹並遺跡]] ({{ill|Yukuhashi|en}}[[ja:行橋市]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]) - Over 1,000 tombs * {{ill|Shiroyama Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:城山横穴群]] ({{ill|Fukuchi, Fukuoka|en}}[[ja:福智町]], Fukuoka) - National Historic Site, over 200 tombs * {{ill|Mizumachi Site|en}}[[ja:水町遺跡]] ({{ill|Nōgata|en}}[[ja:直方市]], Fukuoka) - Prefectural Historic Site, over 40 tombs * {{ill|Kozuki Yokoguchi|en}}[[ja:古月横穴]] ({{ill|Kurate, Fukuoka|en}}[[ja:鞍手町]], Fukuoka) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Ishinuki Nagino Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:石貫ナギノ横穴群]] ({{ill|Tamana|en}}[[ja:玉名市]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Ishinuki Anakanon Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:石貫穴観音横穴群]] (Tamana, Kumamoto) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Nabeta Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:鍋田横穴群]] ({{ill|Yamaga, Kumamoto|en}}[[ja:山鹿市]], Kumamoto) - National Historic Site, 60 tombs * {{ill|Ōmura Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:大村横穴群]] ({{ill|Hitoyoshi|en}}[[ja:人吉市]], Kumamoto) - National Historic Site, 27 tombs * {{ill|Yokkaichi Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:四日市横穴群]] ({{ill|Usa|en}}[[ja:宇佐市]], [[Ōita Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, 161 tombs * {{ill|Takio Hyakka Yokoguchi Kofun Cluster|en}}[[ja:滝尾百穴横穴古墳群]] ({{ill|Ōita, Ōita|en}}[[ja:大分市]], Ōita) - Municipal Historic Site, 75 tombs * {{ill|Hasunogaike Yokoguchi Cluster|en}}[[ja:蓮ヶ池横穴群]] ({{ill|Miyazaki, Miyazaki|en}}[[ja:宮崎市]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site
== Related Articles == * [[Kofun period]] * [[Decorated kofun|Decorated Kofun]]
== References == * Nagahara, Keiji (1999). Iwanami Japanese History Dictionary. Iwanami Shoten. * Yokohama History Foundation (2013). History of Yokohama Tombs.

'''Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Tomb Group''' (楊谷寺谷戸横穴群), also known as '''Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group''' (楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群) or '''Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Burial Group''', is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Ōiso, Naka District, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It has been designated as a Kanagawa Prefecture Historic Site.[[File:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群11号墓.jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Yōkokuji Valley Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群]] Tomb 11: House-shaped chamber.]] [[ja:吉見百穴]] [[ja:柏谷横穴群]] '''Yokohama Tombs''' (横穴墓, Yokōbo/Ouketsubo<ref name="rekihaku">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=https://www.rekihaku.city.yokohama.jp/maibun/qa/detail.php?seq=22|title=Q：横穴墓ってなんて読むのですか?|publisher=Yokohama City Foundation for History and Culture|accessdate=2018-07-28]]</ref>) are burial tombs generally carved into slopes or cliffs, typically measuring around 2 meters in height and extending several meters in depth. These tombs served as burial chambers in ancient East Asia and are most commonly associated with the [[Kofun period]] in Japan, where this burial type emerged and became widely used.

Investigations were conducted in [[1879]] (Meiji 12) and [[1962]] (Shōwa 37).
This burial group features a variety of architectural styles, beginning with tomb 11, which has a square layout with a gabled-roof design. This evolved into tomb 5, which has a dome-shaped ceiling—a simplified version of the hip-and-gable roof. == Distribution and Notable Yokoguchi Tombs == These tombs are found from Kyushu through San'in, Kinki, Hokuriku, and Tokai, with a high concentration in [[Southern Kantō]]. The northern limit is thought to be in northern [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. Approximately 3,000 examples exist in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]].

'''Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Tomb Group''' (楊谷寺谷戸横穴群), also known as '''Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group''' (楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群) or '''Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Burial Group''', is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Ōiso, Naka District, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It has been designated as a Kanagawa Prefecture Historic Site.[[File:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群11号墓.jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Yōkokuji Valley Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群]] Tomb 11: House-shaped chamber.]] [[ja:吉見百穴]] [[ja:柏谷横穴群]] '''Yokohama Tombs''' (横穴墓, Yokōbo/Ouketsubo<ref name="rekihaku">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=https://www.rekihaku.city.yokohama.jp/maibun/qa/detail.php?seq=22|title=Q：横穴墓ってなんて読むのですか?|publisher=Yokohama City Foundation for History and Culture|accessdate=2018-07-28]]</ref>) are burial tombs generally carved into slopes or cliffs, typically measuring around 2 meters in height and extending several meters in depth. These tombs served as burial chambers in ancient East Asia and are most commonly associated with the [[Kofun period]] in Japan, where this burial type emerged and became widely used.
== Overview == The tomb group is situated on the southern slope of the ridges in the [[Ōiso Hills]], an area known for having one of Japan’s highest concentrations of rock-cut tombs. Located in a tributary valley of [[Koma Mountain]], 27 tombs have been identified, spread across four tiers (seven of which have been buried by soil deposition){{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}<ref name="Explanation Board Kanagawa"/>. Investigations were conducted in [[1879]] (Meiji 12) and [[1962]] (Shōwa 37).
This burial group features a variety of architectural styles, beginning with tomb 11, which has a square layout with a gabled-roof design. This evolved into tomb 5, which has a dome-shaped ceiling—a simplified version of the hip-and-gable roof. Further simplifications resulted in the arch-shaped ceilings of tombs 4 and 7<ref name="Explanation Board Kanagawa">Historic Site Explanation Board (Kanagawa Prefecture Board of Education, 1982).</ref>. Due to looting, only fragmented grave goods remain{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}. As one of the most well-known and representative burial groups in the Ōiso Hills, it is considered significant for studying the structural evolution of rock-cut tombs{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}<ref name="Explanation Board Ōiso Town">Historic Site Explanation Board (Ōiso Town Board of Education, 2010).</ref>.
In [[1966]] (Shōwa 41), the site was designated a Historic Site by Kanagawa Prefecture.
== Excavation History == * [[1879]] (Meiji 12): Investigated by [[Tsuboi Shōgorō]]{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group (Kofun)|1989}}. * [[1962]] (Shōwa 37): Survey conducted by [[Akaboshi Naotada]] and the Ōiso Town Board of Education, with a report published in [[1964]]{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group (Kofun)|1989}}. * [[1966]] (Shōwa 41), July 19: Designated as a Kanagawa Prefecture Historic Site<ref name="Cultural Property List"/>.

== Cultural Property == === Kanagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === * Historic Site ** '''Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Tomb Group''' - Designated on July 19, [[1966]] (Shōwa 41)<ref name="Cultural Property List">{{PDFlink|[http://www.town.oiso.kanagawa.jp/material/files/group/30/bunkazai21218.pdf Designated Cultural Properties List]}} (Ōiso Town Website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Ōiso Town Local History Museum''' (Nishi-Oiso, Ōiso Town, within the Kanagawa Prefectural Ōiso Shiroyama Park) - Stores and displays artifacts from Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group.

[[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese burial practices]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Rock-cut tomb cluster]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Corridor-type kofun]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:kk]] [[ja:横穴墓]] [[Category:Corridor-type kofun]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Corridor-type kofun forks]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:kk]] {{Kofun navbar}}[[Category:kk]] [[Category:fixing the kk issue]]

'''Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Tomb Group''' (楊谷寺谷戸横穴群), also known as '''Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group''' (楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群) or '''Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Burial Group''', is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Ōiso, Naka District, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It has been designated as a Kanagawa Prefecture Historic Site.[[File:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群11号墓.jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Yōkokuji Valley Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群]] Tomb 11: House-shaped chamber.]] [[ja:吉見百穴]] [[ja:柏谷横穴群]] '''Yokohama Tombs''' (横穴墓, Yokōbo/Ouketsubo<ref name="rekihaku">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=https://www.rekihaku.city.yokohama.jp/maibun/qa/detail.php?seq=22|title=Q：横穴墓ってなんて読むのですか?|publisher=Yokohama City Foundation for History and Culture|accessdate=2018-07-28]]</ref>) are burial tombs generally carved into slopes or cliffs, typically measuring around 2 meters in height and extending several meters in depth. These tombs served as burial chambers in ancient East Asia and are most commonly associated with the [[Kofun period]] in Japan, where this burial type emerged and became widely used.
== Overview == The tomb group is situated on the southern slope of the ridges in the [[Ōiso Hills]], an area known for having one of Japan’s highest concentrations of rock-cut tombs. Located in a tributary valley of [[Koma Mountain]], 27 tombs have been identified, spread across four tiers (seven of which have been buried by soil deposition){{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}<ref name="Explanation Board Kanagawa"/>. Investigations were conducted in [[1879]] (Meiji 12) and [[1962]] (Shōwa 37).
This burial group features a variety of architectural styles, beginning with tomb 11, which has a square layout with a gabled-roof design. This evolved into tomb 5, which has a dome-shaped ceiling—a simplified version of the hip-and-gable roof. Further simplifications resulted in the arch-shaped ceilings of tombs 4 and 7<ref name="Explanation Board Kanagawa">Historic Site Explanation Board (Kanagawa Prefecture Board of Education, 1982).</ref>. Due to looting, only fragmented grave goods remain{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}. As one of the most well-known and representative burial groups in the Ōiso Hills, it is considered significant for studying the structural evolution of rock-cut tombs{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}<ref name="Explanation Board Ōiso Town">Historic Site Explanation Board (Ōiso Town Board of Education, 2010).</ref>.
In [[1966]] (Shōwa 41), the site was designated a Historic Site by Kanagawa Prefecture.
== Excavation History == * [[1879]] (Meiji 12): Investigated by [[Tsuboi Shōgorō]]{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group (Kofun)|1989}}. * [[1962]] (Shōwa 37): Survey conducted by [[Akaboshi Naotada]] and the Ōiso Town Board of Education, with a report published in [[1964]]{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group (Kofun)|1989}}. * [[1966]] (Shōwa 41), July 19: Designated as a Kanagawa Prefecture Historic Site<ref name="Cultural Property List"/>.
== Major Rock-Cut Tombs == {| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;white-space:nowrap;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |- ! Tomb !! Entrance !! Interior |- |'''Tomb 11''' |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-11-1.jpg|200px]] |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-11-2.jpg|200px]] |- |'''Tomb 5''' |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-5-1.jpg|200px]] |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-5-2.jpg|200px]] |- |'''Tomb 4''' |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-4-1.jpg|200px]] |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-4-2.jpg|200px]] |- |'''Tomb 7''' |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-7-1.jpg|200px]] |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-7-2.jpg|200px]] |- |}
== Cultural Property == === Kanagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === * Historic Site ** '''Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Tomb Group''' - Designated on July 19, [[1966]] (Shōwa 41)<ref name="Cultural Property List">{{PDFlink|[http://www.town.oiso.kanagawa.jp/material/files/group/30/bunkazai21218.pdf Designated Cultural Properties List]}} (Ōiso Town Website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Ōiso Town Local History Museum''' (Nishi-Oiso, Ōiso Town, within the Kanagawa Prefectural Ōiso Shiroyama Park) - Stores and displays artifacts from Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group.

== Distribution and Notable Yokoguchi Tombs == These tombs are found from Kyushu through San'in, Kinki, Hokuriku, and Tokai, with a high concentration in [[Southern Kantō]]. The northern limit is thought to be in northern [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. Approximately 3,000 examples exist in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]].

'''Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Tomb Group''' (楊谷寺谷戸横穴群), also known as '''Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group''' (楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群) or '''Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Burial Group''', is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Ōiso, Naka District, Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It has been designated as a Kanagawa Prefecture Historic Site.[[File:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群11号墓.jpg|250px|right|thumb|{{ill|Yōkokuji Valley Yokana Cluster|en}}[[ja:楊谷寺谷戸横穴墓群]] Tomb 11: House-shaped chamber.]] [[ja:吉見百穴]] [[ja:柏谷横穴群]] '''Yokohama Tombs''' (横穴墓, Yokōbo/Ouketsubo<ref name="rekihaku">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=https://www.rekihaku.city.yokohama.jp/maibun/qa/detail.php?seq=22|title=Q：横穴墓ってなんて読むのですか?|publisher=Yokohama City Foundation for History and Culture|accessdate=2018-07-28]]</ref>) are burial tombs generally carved into slopes or cliffs, typically measuring around 2 meters in height and extending several meters in depth. These tombs served as burial chambers in ancient East Asia and are most commonly associated with the [[Kofun period]] in Japan, where this burial type emerged and became widely used.
== Overview == The tomb group is situated on the southern slope of the ridges in the [[Ōiso Hills]], an area known for having one of Japan’s highest concentrations of rock-cut tombs. Located in a tributary valley of [[Koma Mountain]], 27 tombs have been identified, spread across four tiers (seven of which have been buried by soil deposition){{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}<ref name="Explanation Board Kanagawa"/>. Investigations were conducted in [[1879]] (Meiji 12) and [[1962]] (Shōwa 37).
This burial group features a variety of architectural styles, beginning with tomb 11, which has a square layout with a gabled-roof design. This evolved into tomb 5, which has a dome-shaped ceiling—a simplified version of the hip-and-gable roof. Further simplifications resulted in the arch-shaped ceilings of tombs 4 and 7<ref name="Explanation Board Kanagawa">Historic Site Explanation Board (Kanagawa Prefecture Board of Education, 1982).</ref>. Due to looting, only fragmented grave goods remain{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}. As one of the most well-known and representative burial groups in the Ōiso Hills, it is considered significant for studying the structural evolution of rock-cut tombs{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group (Heibonsha)|1984}}<ref name="Explanation Board Ōiso Town">Historic Site Explanation Board (Ōiso Town Board of Education, 2010).</ref>.
In [[1966]] (Shōwa 41), the site was designated a Historic Site by Kanagawa Prefecture.
== Excavation History == * [[1879]] (Meiji 12): Investigated by [[Tsuboi Shōgorō]]{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group (Kofun)|1989}}. * [[1962]] (Shōwa 37): Survey conducted by [[Akaboshi Naotada]] and the Ōiso Town Board of Education, with a report published in [[1964]]{{Sfn|Yōkokuji-Yato Rock-Cut Tomb Group (Kofun)|1989}}. * [[1966]] (Shōwa 41), July 19: Designated as a Kanagawa Prefecture Historic Site<ref name="Cultural Property List"/>.
== Major Rock-Cut Tombs == {| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;white-space:nowrap;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |- ! Tomb !! Entrance !! Interior |- |'''Tomb 11''' |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-11-1.jpg|200px]] |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-11-2.jpg|200px]] |- |'''Tomb 5''' |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-5-1.jpg|200px]] |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-5-2.jpg|200px]] |- |'''Tomb 4''' |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-4-1.jpg|200px]] |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-4-2.jpg|200px]] |- |'''Tomb 7''' |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-7-1.jpg|200px]] |[[File:Yokokuji-yato Yokoana Group-7-2.jpg|200px]] |- |}
== Cultural Property == === Kanagawa Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === * Historic Site ** '''Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Tomb Group''' - Designated on July 19, [[1966]] (Shōwa 41)<ref name="Cultural Property List">{{PDFlink|[http://www.town.oiso.kanagawa.jp/material/files/group/30/bunkazai21218.pdf Designated Cultural Properties List]}} (Ōiso Town Website).</ref>.
== Related Facilities == * '''Ōiso Town Local History Museum''' (Nishi-Oiso, Ōiso Town, within the Kanagawa Prefectural Ōiso Shiroyama Park) - Stores and displays artifacts from Yōkokuji-Yato Yokouana Group.
== References == {{reflist}}

== Related Literature == {{small|(Literature not directly used for this article)}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1964|title=Rock-Cut Tombs of Ōiso, Kanagawa Prefecture (Ōiso Town Cultural Property Survey Report Vol. 1)|publisher=Ōiso Town Board of Education|ref=}} * {{Cite journal|author=Sanpei Yūji, Kashiwagi Zenji|title=Structure of House-Shaped Rock-Cut Tombs in Yōkokuji-Yato: A Study on Three-Dimensional Measurement and Morphological Analysis|date=2022|journal=Jōhō Kōkogaku|publisher=Nihon Jōhō Kōkogaku-kai|volume=27|number=1-2|pages=22-35|ref=}}
